Learning Objectives

● Define and describe archaeological surveys, GIS, and Remote Sensing  
● Identify the interdisciplinary sciences needed to collect usable geospatial data from pedestrian surveys and public datasets  
● Analyze the use of GIS and related technologies and suggest situations where specific applications would be needed  
● Explore the use of GIS within specific archaeological projects

In 2011, my husband, James Katsutoshi Malta, had just graduated from the University of Colorado at Colorado Springs with a degree in archaeology. His first professional job was with a CRM (Cultural Resource Management) firm with the title “staff scientist.” This meant he was in charge of collecting artifacts from sites and analyzing them for the company. The best part of the job? In his words, it was getting to say, “We’ve got to get this back to the lab!” But, how did James find the [artifacts](#term_843_1261) and what did he do with the information once his analysis was done? (Some may also wonder, how did he survive the eye rolls from the rest of the survey team when he yelled “We’ve got to get this back to the lab!” for the fifth time in 10 minutes?) The answer, at least to the first question, is by conducting surveys and using the geospatial data to provide the clients with a robust analysis of which areas were safe to begin construction in and which areas needed more investigation first, in order to comply with federal, state, and local laws.

Archaeological surveys are used to identify potential [sites](#term_843_58), locate artifacts, and provide the raw data for more complex interpretations. For example, finding similar artifacts across a large geographic area can help us to figure out what ancient trade networks might have been used, how ancient economies functioned, or how people were using lands. Traditional surveys, more commonly known as [pedestrian surveys](#term_843_844), require physical presence at a location and specific procedures to look for artifacts on the surface of the ground. These surveys are vital to the entire process of site identification and excavation. However, surveys alone are not sufficient to get an idea of what might be present. Archaeologists have long relied on mapping of these individual artifacts to determine the extent of the site. Modern technology has allowed for the integration of survey data into larger archaeological workflows, giving us much greater speed, precision, and accuracy even when full excavations are not possible or the extent of an archaeological site is not known.

The blending of traditional mapping with modern technology has resulted in the emergence of [Geographic Information Systems](#term_843_1263) (GIS) and [Geographic Information Science](#term_843_1264) (GIScience). The GIS focuses more on the software and hardware used, while GIScience focuses more on the applications of those technologies to scientific disciplines. For simplicity, this chapter will use GIS as a generic term to refer to both the systems and science.

GIS is not a single method, concept, or program. Instead it is a way of organizing, displaying, and analyzing data that has some type of geospatial component. This interdisciplinary science has been applied to fields from cultural anthropology to urban planning, wildlife management to mining, and economics to anthropology, among others. In today’s world, GIS is often intimately tied with [Remote Sensing](#term_843_1265) (RS), which is a set of methods that allows for the collection of data without anyone being physically present at a location and without needing to directly observe the objects being studied.

This chapter will examine the uses of GIS and Remote Sensing in the field of archaeology and demonstrate how they complement more traditional methods of surveying and mapping. Not only can these techniques be used to understand past peoples, but they can help us preserve the cultural heritage we have inherited from past generations. We can use these techniques to analyze the movement of ancient peoples, avoid destroying sites during development, and even predict the precise location of previously undiscovered sites.

## Surveys, Software, and Statistics

Pedestrian surveys are a cornerstone of archaeology. These surveys rely on large teams of archaeologists and volunteers to walk a site in a specific pattern. Generally, individuals start by lining up at one end of an area of interest, approximately two meters (just over six feet) apart (Figure 1). However, the exact distance between individuals depends on the [research design](#term_843_661), the amount of ground cover (plants, snow, etc.), and the logistics of the project. CRM projects will generally need to be conducted on a tight budget with limited time, so their pedestrian surveys may use a spacing of 10 meters to 20 meters, depending on the requirements of the state. On the other hand, a well-funded excavation at a known (and protected) archaeological site will stick to the two-meter rule, since the cost-benefit trade-off is less of a concern; the researchers “know” they will find important artifacts. The team then slowly walks across the field together, carefully scanning the ground for any potential artifacts. Anytime something is found, the team will pause while the item is examined, GPS coordinates are taken, and the item is “tagged and bagged.” This involves completing a standardized form that includes information like the date, GPS coordinates, individual who identified the object, and any contextual information that may be relevant (see [these forms](https://archeology.uark.edu/forms-records/) from the University of Arkansas).

Pedestrian surveys are often accompanied by [systematic](#term_843_1266) excavations of [shovel test pits](#term_843_1267) (STP). These are generally laid out in a grid pattern over the area where pedestrian surveys have been completed. As the name implies, shovels (or other excavation tools) are used to dig test holes, regardless of whether artifacts have been found on the surface. Test pits are generally around 30 cm to 50 cm (around 12 inches to 20 inches) in diameter and up to around 1 m (just over three feet) in depth, though the specifics of the site and likely age of the artifacts can require changes to these general guidelines (Figure 2). Some artifacts are too small to be immediately identified, so screens are used to sift through the excavated material to ensure small artifacts are not missed (for more details on this, see the excavation chapter). This allows the researchers to systematically assess where (and if) further excavation is needed. Within the CRM context, STPs may be used in areas where the pedestrian surveys would be obscured by ground cover (like plants or snow), or where local jurisdictions require STPs by law. STPs are sometimes skipped entirely in areas of low likelihood of finding archaeological materials, since digging takes time and can increase the length of the project. Similarly, some areas don’t allow for STPs to be conducted, such as in areas with exposed bedrock or over (geologically) recent lava flows in Hawaii.

Mapping is an essential skill for any archaeologist, both interpreting maps made by others and creating them ourselves. The first step to reading a map prepared by a professional mapmaker (cartographer) is to determine the directions displayed. This is called orientation and typically is accomplished by finding the arrow pointing north (or a compass rose). The next step to reading a map is to determine the scale, or amount that the world has been shrunk to fit within the image. For example, on a 1 cm:100 m scale, each one centimeter on the printed map represents 100 meters of the real world.

In Figure 3, the compass rose indicates that “up” is west and “right” is north. This may seem strange at first if you are accustomed to “up” being north. For context, the sign in Figure 3 is physically attached to a metal grate, so that anyone reading the sign right side up would be facing the west. Historic maps tended to use east as “up.” The term orientation comes from “The Orient,” which is east from a European perspective.  Figure 3 does not contain a separate scale, but it still indicates how far major cities are from the observer in kilometers (km).

Rough hand maps are typically made during any survey and help the archaeologists to envision what is occurring at the site. These are often digitized, either by snapping a photo of the hand-drawn map or using applications like Google Images or Microsoft Paint to re-create the hand-drawn map (for a great example of this, see Figure 2 in [Cultural Resource Management and Archaeology: Conserving Heritage](https://textbooks.whatcom.edu/tracesarchaeology/chapter/archaeological-surveys-and-remote-sensing/?preview_id=843&preview_nonce=3e800da8b5&preview=true#chapter-)). Few archaeologists are also artists, so our hand maps tend to be very rough and not to scale. They are vital for field notes and keeping track of what is occurring during any survey, but thankfully technology can help make our rough sketches more accurate and presentable to others (see the ArcGIS and QGIS section below).

Once the data from surveys and STPs are collected, and any artifacts appropriately curated, archaeologists then go about converting the pen-and-paper records into digital records. Most of the time, this requires inputting all the information into a spreadsheet, like a Microsoft Excel or LibreOffice sheet (Figure 4), but some archaeologists prefer adding things to Google Sheets. Eventually, these files are compiled into entire databases, such as with Microsoft Access or OpenOffice Base (but those details are best left to data scientists, not archaeologists). Regardless of the specific program used, the data must be entered in specific columns and generally needs to be in numeric format (so that the spreadsheet can be easily used with other programs—discussed in the following paragraphs). Many archaeologists are now skipping the conversion from pen-and-paper records by recording data directly into a digital format while still in the field. This has become easier with applications like Microsoft Forms or Google Forms, as well as with more specialized mobile applications. Keep in mind that those kinds of applications are a more convenient way of accomplishing the same task of organizing data into columns and rows.

### Statistical Software

Statistical analyses are needed for most archaeological work. This can take a variety of forms, from identifying differences in surface features to finding small changes in the frequency of artifacts (see the Ancient Technologies and Ancient Environment chapters for more on this). Sometimes this involves just counting objects, but sometimes it involves more advanced statistical calculations, from t-tests to ANOVAs (analysis of variance) and beyond. If you’re not comfortable doing math by hand, don’t worry! That’s why archaeologists put data into spreadsheets—so the computer will do the math for us.

Two of the most common statistical programs used by researchers are SPSS and PSPP. SPSS stands for the “Statistical Package for the Social Sciences” and is offered by IBM. SPSS subscriptions are quite affordable while attending college (around $80 per year, as of 2024), but quickly skyrocket for professionals (around $100 per month, as of 2024). To avoid this, and to ensure equity for people of all income levels, many professionals switch to open-source alternatives. PSPP isn’t a fancy abbreviation, but instead a play on SPSS. PSPP offers most of the common features of SPSS, including a graphical interface and options for coding more advanced statistics, but without any financial investment! In fact, you can download PSPP right now, for free, from <https://www.gnu.org/software/pspp>. The open software movement is vital to creating a more equitable research environment, as are any efforts that make research activities more widely available for both the producers and consumers of knowledge.

Another common approach is to use R and RStudio. R is a programming language developed for statistical analyses and is used by RStudio to run many statistical tests. RStudio provides a GUI (Graphical User Interface), but still requires knowledge of how to write code in R. For more on how R and RStudio works, see the [official RStudio website](https://posit.co/products/open-source/rstudio/).

### Geospatial Software

Once the archaeologist has entered the data into spreadsheets and run any statistics that are needed, the next step is to put the data into GIS software. The two most common GIS programs used are ArcGIS and QGIS. Just like with SPSS and PSPP, one requires a paid subscription while the other is free and open-source. ArcGIS is the industry standard (and priced accordingly), while QGIS is the open-source alternative. Both systems work best with data in .csv format, but any spreadsheet format can be used. Data must be imported into the program and tools run to convert the raw data from the spreadsheet into data that the GIS software can display. This most often involves using GPS data, which may come from pedestrian surveys or STPs. The GIS tools create a layer, or an overlay of data onto a background, for us to visually inspect. See the Working With Spreadsheets and QGIS box for an example of how this is done in QGIS.

Once we have the data in our GIS program and created the layer of our data, we can start mapping out the archaeological site. Archaeologists often create multiple maps to display the information in different ways for different audiences and to highlight different findings. For example, it might be important to show the path of the pedestrian survey on one map and the number of artifacts found in each STP on another map. The process of creating maps showing specific information is called cartography. The principles of cartography are beyond the scope of this chapter, but an example of the kinds of maps created are presented in the Working With Spreadsheets and QGIS box.

GIS programs are not only used for creating pretty maps—they are much more powerful than that. They can be used by archaeologists at every step of the archaeological process, from planning to the collection and analysis of data and even in the peer review process. For example, each program has a wide array of tools that can be used to do things like plan new pedestrian surveys, create buffers around features like houses or roads, or analyze the topography of an area to predict where artifacts might be located. Unlike in past decades, this last use of GIS programs is now intimately tied with aircraft and remote sensing.

Working With Spreadsheets and QGIS

This section demonstrates how to use open-source data and QGIS to create a map of archaeological finds. In this example, data were obtained from Open Context (<https://opencontext.org/>) regarding the location, amount, and type of archaeological finds. This is tied in with the CowQueLatte activity, linked here.

This first pair of images shows the CowQueLatte spreadsheet open in Microsoft Excel and LibreOffice Calc. In both programs, the data is identical—it’s only the display that differs between the programs. Whenever using data, it’s best to open the file in a spreadsheet viewer (MS Excel, LO Calc, or Google Sheets) to check that the data are correct and accurate.

The next step, after ensuring the .csv file contains the correct data, is to open QGIS. This image represents the QGIS home page when first opening the program. For this example, the chapter author selected “New Empty Project.”

Now that a QGIS project has been started, we need to add the data (from the CowQueLatte.csv spreadsheet) to our QGIS project. This is done by selecting Layer -> Add Layer -> Add Delimited Text Layer.

Clicking on “Add Delimited Text Layer” brings up the “Data Source Manager” pop-up box set to the “Delimited Text” option on the left-hand side. This allows us to select “CSV” as the file format before we select the CowQueLatte.csv file by clicking on the three dots after the long “File Name” box.

Selecting the “CowQueLatte.csv” file brings up additional options in the Data Source Manager box. We are mostly concerned with telling QGIS how and where the location of each archaeological find is stored in the spreadsheet. Fortunately, the “CowQueLatte.csv” file already has the Latitude and Longitude saved as specific columns. We can therefore just select “Longitude” in the X field and “Latitude” in the Y field. Remember—longitude lines are numbered from east to west and west to east, while latitude lines are numbered from south to north and north to south.

After clicking “Add,” QGIS now has the information to put a point at each latitude and longitude location where an artifact was found. QGIS converted our spreadsheet to a layer of points, with the layer title CowQueLatte in the bottom-left corner.

Having the location of each archaeological site is important, but so is having some kind of reference layer so that we can tell the artifacts don’t exist in a white void. To add a base layer, we can select Web -> QuickMapServices -> OSM -> OSM Standard.

Adding the OSM Standard layer gives us a good reference for where each of these archaeological finds were located. The lower-right corner shows Milano (in English, we call this city Milan, Italy) while the center of the map shows Schweiz/Suisse/Svizzera/Svizra (the names for Switzerland in the official languages of German/French/Italian/Romansh, respectively).

Now that we have all of the archaeological finds displayed and a reference layer, you might be wondering what happened to all the other information stored in the spreadsheet. We can see the data stored for each location by selecting the “Identify Features” button in the middle of the top ribbon under “Processing,” or by entering “Ctrl + Shift + I” on the keyboard.

Clicking on any of the points will select the data for that one individual archaeological find. In this case, we have selected the site near the center of the screen. When we click on it, the color changes from green to red to let us know that we have selected that particular site. The bottom-right corner of the screen also changes to show us all the data stored in the spreadsheet for that row.

Now, let’s change how we see each of the sites visualized on the map. To do this, we right-click on the CowQueLatte layer we created in the bottom-left corner of the screen. Then, we click on “Properties.”

The “Layer Properties” pop-up window allows us to make many different changes to the selected layer. Right now, we’re only concerned with changing how we see the locations represented on the map. This is called the layer’s “Symbology.” The layers have a single symbol by default, with a random color assigned by QGIS when we create the layer. In this example, the color is green, but it could be any color.

We often need to display more information than just the location of each archaeological site. For example, we might need to show when each site was occupied by people, how many artifacts were found, or what type of artifacts were found. In this example, we’ll change the color so that we can quickly see the latest age for each of the finds. This can be done by either changing the “Single Symbol” box to Graduated or Categorized. Graduated will bring up a continuous gradient but won’t let us quickly group sites of similar ages together.

Instead, we select “Categorized” and then need to tell QGIS what “Value” we want the different symbols to be based on. In this example, we’re trying to show the most recent occupation of each site, so we’ll select the “LateBCE/CE” field.

Now we have told QGIS that we are going to choose specific values of “LateBCE/CE” to show as different colors. QGIS has created a whole list of the possible values for “LateBCE/CE” and assigned each one a random color. Since we started off with all the sites shown in a green color, we’re going to change the “Color ramp” by clicking on the box that currently says “Random Colors” and selecting “Greens.” We also need to shrink down the number of categories QGIS is putting the “LateBCE/CE” values into by clicking on “Classify” in the lower left-hand corner. For our example, we decided to classify five categories of “Late BCE/CE.”

Once the color ramp is set to green and five classes have been set up, we click “Apply” and can see this map. You can see that each point is now one of five colors, giving us this view.

Before we finish our map, we need to change the OSM Standard layer. The green values are fine by themselves but can easily be lost in the green background. For this, we just right-click on the “OSM Standard” and change the view to black-and-white.

There are several additional steps we can take in QGIS to create a full map (via the Project—New Print Layout options). The details of how to do this can be found elsewhere, but we are able to create this map showing the latest calculated age of each archaeological find.

Maps like this are crucial to making sense out of large-scale archaeological data. Here, we created a map showing where people were likely to encounter bovines (cows and their relatives). This helps us to interpret the results of any statistical analyses and communicate those findings to the public.

## Aerospace, Aircraft, and Archaeology

None of the GIS programs could work without advances in aerospace engineering. Specifically, the Global Positioning System (GPS) satellites allow anyone to map the world around them down to the meter mark, with some systems allowing for millimeter accuracy (<0.04 inches). Those in use by the general public, such as the GPS in the device you may be using to read this textbook, are somewhat less accurate at approximately 5 m (just over 16 feet). The accuracy may be increased substantially if there are multiple systems running at once or decreased if there are environmental hazards, such as buildings, mountains, or trees.

GPS satellites are run largely by the national governments of the world, though private companies are beginning to enter the market as well. These systems are therefore more accurate in countries with ties to the programs, such as in the United States. Unfortunately, there are often fewer satellites for use in remote areas or in the Global South, resulting in less accuracy in these areas.

Other satellite programs are in use to continually map the world around us. One particularly useful program is the Landsat program, run by the United States’ National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) and the United States Geological Survey (USGS). The ninth iteration of this program was fast-tracked for launch in September of 2021 and ensures the continued creation of publicly available data. In fact, you could use this [link](https://earthexplorer.usgs.gov/) to create an account and download the data for the area you’re sitting in right now.

If you were to download this image, however, you would immediately find that you couldn’t see yourself or necessarily make out the features of your immediate environment. Instead, you would find that each pixel represents an area of 900 m2(~1,075 ft2), or around 100 ft2, larger than the average apartment in the U.S. This resolution is not particularly useful for identifying individual homes or people, but is excellent for getting an idea of the ground cover or geographic features of a large area.

Atmospheric distortions, like clouds, wind, or air currents, make greater resolution very difficult. Light must pass through the atmosphere twice, once to reach the ground and reflect off the surface and then a second time to reach the satellite in orbit. Even tiny changes in air pressure, temperature, or wind speed can bend the light enough to make a high resolution image impossible to obtain. Unfortunately, satellites cannot stop and wait for conditions to improve; they must continue their orbital fall around Earth.

Improvements in other areas of technology, however, have allowed us to bring the satellites much closer to home. Aerial images were in use long before the first artificial satellite, the USSR’s Sputnik, was launched into orbit. Images from airplanes and helicopters allow for much greater resolution and ease of access, since it is much cheaper and easier to fly a plane than a rocket. Archaeologists have long used aerial photography to map sites, conduct surveys, and even to make the famous discovery of the Nazca lines in Peru (Kosok, 1959; Ruggles & Saunders, 2012). The USGS’s EROS archive contains aerial images covering the entire continental United States with each pixel equating to approximately 1 m (3.28 ft) (USGS, n.d.). Specific flights and projects have frequently reduced the pixel size to approximately 15 cm (5.90 in.) (USGS, n.d.). Unfortunately, the utility of this type of aerial imaging is limited due to the relatively high cost of chartering airplanes and the potential distances of many sites from civilian airports.

### Regulations, Radar, and Remote Sensing

The past 20 years have seen the rise of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), also known as drones or Unmanned Aerial Systems (UAS). The smaller size of UAVs means that they consume significantly less fuel than a full-sized airplane, with many models running only on battery power (see Figure 6). This reduction in fuel needs means much cheaper, more frequent imaging that can be tailored to specific concerns. Even better for those in the United States, the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) has created a special type of pilot license for professional UAV pilots. By using this type of license, anyone over the age of 16 can fly a UAV and gather their own imagery. This is often called a “Part 107” pilot license, referring to a specific federal code that allows these pilots to fly UAVs, but not manned aircraft like planes or helicopters.

It is important to note that “manned” and “unmanned” do not refer to the gender or sex of the pilot carried aboard the aircraft. These are older terms that were developed using the term “man” as a gender-neutral term for a human. More inclusive language has yet to be put into widespread use within the aviation industry, but pilots can be just as diverse as any other profession.

The ability to specifically target flights on field sites allows archaeologists to gain all the benefits of aerial imaging without the high costs of launching satellites or chartering planes. We also can fly multiple times, often on multiple days, meaning weather is much less of a concern. These detailed data collections can be used in combination with GIS and Remote Sensing software (discussed later in this chapter) to create detailed maps and 3D models and even reveal features otherwise hidden by vegetation. One example of this is the [Vanderbilt Initiative for Interdisciplinary Geospatial Research](https://www.vanderbilt.edu/viigr/), which is using data gathered from various UAVs to create [virtual reality](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rpOFqIqQtiY) models of Andean archaeological sites (Wolf 2021). This project uses not only data collected by the researchers themselves, but also from an entire community of researchers via the Geospatial Platform for Andean Culture, History, and Archaeology (Wernke et al. 2024).

As mentioned in the introduction to this chapter, Remote Sensing refers to many different techniques that allow us to detect objects that cannot be detected from our senses alone. Some of these techniques require us to be physically present near a location, some require a device to be controlled remotely, such as a UAV, and some techniques require a satellite in orbit around Earth. These techniques often involve the use of electromagnetic waves (see Figure 7) or sound waves, but can also use electrical fields, magnetic fields, or even seismic waves.

The first technique developed was [sonar](#term_843_1268) (SOund Navigation And Ranging). Sonar was first used to help ships navigate and avoid collisions. The simplest form of sonar sends out a burst of sound waves (called a “ping”) and someone listens for the echo of this “ping.” The time between “ping” and echo is used to calculate the distance to the object. And motion is detected by doppler shift, which causes a higher-pitched “ping” to be heard if the object is moving toward the listener, while a lower-pitched “ping” is heard if the object is moving away from the listener. This can help archaeologists find the location of sunken ships, buildings, or other human-made objects from the comfort (and relative safety) of the surface. Many Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs) have been equipped with a combination of sonar and radar to more effectively map the ocean floor, leading to many major discoveries (see <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8qCZ4CTs2pc> and <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BRlhRWShfJY>).

One of the most commonly used remote sensing techniques used is [radar](#term_843_1281) (**RA**dio **D**etection **A**nd **R**anging). Radar uses radio waves to detect the size, position, and motion of objects by bouncing different wavelengths off anything within the detector’s range. The proce

enriched_points.expanded_content: Remote sensing techniques like GPR, LiDAR, and satellite imagery enhance archaeological site discovery, enabling non-invasive exploration of landscapes and structures previously hidden beneath vegetation or soil.

Case studies reveal significant archaeological finds, including unmarked graves using GPR and extensive ancient settlements identified via LiDAR, showcasing the transformative impact of remote sensing.
Remote sensing has revolutionized archaeological methodologies, allowing for greater efficiency, accuracy in mapping, and preservation of sites, while reducing the need for invasive excavation.
